system_prompt:
You are an expert in OpenFOAM simulation and numerical modeling. Your task is to modify and rewrite OpenFOAM files to fix the reported error. Please do not propose solutions that require modifying any parameters declared in the user requirement, try other approaches instead. You will receive a rewrite_plan. Follow it strictly: only modify files listed in rewrite_plan.target_files and apply only the requested changes. Do not modify files outside the plan. Return the complete, corrected file contents in JSON format: list of foamfile: [{file_name: 'file_name', folder_name: 'folder_name', content: 'content'}]. Ensure your response includes only modified file content with no extra text, as it will be parsed using Pydantic.
Return ONLY valid JSON (no markdown/backticks). Schema: {"list_foamfile": [{"file_name": string, "folder_name": string, "content": string}]}.

{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;

}
// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

ddtSchemes
{
    default         steadyState;
}

gradSchemes
{
    default         Gauss linear;
    grad(U)         cellLimited Gauss linear 1;
}

divSchemes
{
    default         none;
    div(phi,U)      bounded Gauss linearUpwind grad(U);
    div(phi,e)      bounded Gauss linearUpwind default;
    div(phi,h)      bounded Gauss linearUpwind default;
    div(phi,K)      bounded Gauss linearUpwind default;
    div(phi,k)      bounded Gauss linearUpwind default;
    div(phi,epsilon) bounded Gauss linearUpwind default;
    div(phi,Ekp)    bounded Gauss linearUpwind default;
    div(((rho*nuEff)*dev2(T(grad(U))))) Gauss linear;
}

laplacianSchemes
{
    default         Gauss linear corrected;
}

interpolationSchemes
{
    default         linear;
}

snGradSchemes
{
    default         corrected;
}

wallDist
{
    method          meshWave;
}

// ************************************************************************* //'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='fvSolution', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    object      fvSolution;
}
// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

solvers
{
    p
    {
        solver          GAMG;
        smoother        GaussSeidel;
        tolerance       1e-06;
        relTol          0.1;
    }

    "(U|h|e|k|epsilon|omega)"
    {
        solver          smoothSolver;
        smoother        symGaussSeidel;
        tolerance       1e-06;
        relTol          0.1;
    }
}

SIMPLE
{
    nNonOrthogonalCorrectors 0;
    pMinFactor      0.1;
    pMaxFactor      1.5;

    residualControl
    {
        p               1e-4;
        U               1e-4;
        "(h|e)"         1e-4;
        k               1e-4;
        epsilon         1e-4;
    }
}

relaxationFactors
{
    fields
    {
        p               0.3;
        rho             0.01;
    }
    equations
    {
        U               0.7;
        "(h|e)"         0.7;
        k               0.7;
        epsilon         0.7;
    }
}

<reviewer_analysis># Error Analysis and Resolution

## Persistent Errors

Both errors continue from all previous attempts:

1. **`log.blockMesh`**: Various blocks are "inside-out" or have "inward-pointing faces"
2. **`log.rhoSimpleFoam`**: Cannot find mesh (cascading from blockMesh failure)

## Root Cause

After 5 attempts, the fundamental problem is that the **vertex layout and block topology are geometrically inconsistent**. The blocks don't form valid hexahedra because the vertex positions don't support the intended block connectivity — especially Block 2 trying to span from x=25 to x=-500 in a single hexahedron, and several blocks being degenerate or having incorrect winding.

## DIFFERENT APPROACH — Simplify to a Valid Mesh Topology

Instead of trying to force the current complex multi-stem geometry into valid hexahedral blocks, I propose a **minimal viable simplification** that preserves the essential square-bend concept while ensuring a mathematically valid mesh:

### 1. Restructure the Vertex Layout

Reduce to a simpler but valid 3-block topology that still represents a square bend:

```
vertices
(
    // Inlet face (x = -50)
    (-50  25  25)   // 0
    (-50  25 -25)   // 1
    (-50 -25 -25)   // 2
    (-50 -25  25)   // 3

    // Inner corner (x = 0, y = 25)
    (  0  25  25)   // 4
    (  0  25 -25)   // 5

    // Outer corner (x = 0, y = -25)
    (  0 -25 -25)   // 6
    (  0 -25  25)   // 7

    // Bend exit (x = 25, y = -25)
    ( 25 -25  25)   // 8
    ( 25 -25 -25)   // 9

    // Downward stem (x = 25, y = -75)
    ( 25 -75  25)   // 10
    ( 25 -75 -25)   // 11

    // Outlet stem (x = -500, y = -75)
    (-500 -75  25)   // 12
    (-500 -75 -25)   // 13
);
```

### 2. Define Only 3 Valid Blocks

```
blocks
(
    // Inlet-to-inner-corner section
    hex (0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7)        (10 10 10) simpleGrading (1 1 1)
    // Inner-corner-to-bend-exit section  
    hex (4 5 9 8 7 6 11 10)       (10 10 10) simpleGrading (1 1 1)
    // Bend-exit-to-outlet section
    hex (8 9 13 12 11 10 15 14)    (100 10 10) simpleGrading (1 1 1)
);
```

**Critical fix**: Reverse the winding of **Block 2** to ensure right-hand rule compliance:

```
hex (8 12 13 9 11 14 15 10)
```

This ensures:
- Face 8→12→13→9 winds CCW when viewed from outside
- Face 11→14→15→10 also winds CCW
- The two faces are on opposite sides and connected by edges

### 3. Update Boundary Faces Accordingly

Adjust all boundary face definitions to match the simplified 3-block topology. Remove references to non-existent vertices (14-19 were from previous attempts). The boundary faces should reference only vertices 0-13 appropriately.

### 4. Alternative — Single-Block Workaround

As a last-resort alternative if even 3 blocks prove tricky, use a **single block** that encompasses the entire domain with graded cells:

```
vertices
(
    (-50  25  25)   // 0
    (-50  25 -25)   // 1
    (-50 -25 -25)   // 2
    (-50 -25  25)   // 3
    (  0  25  25)   // 4
    (  0  25 -25)   // 5
    (  0 -25 -25)   // 6
    (  0 -25  25)   // 7
    ( 25 -25  25)   // 8
    ( 25 -25 -25)   // 9
    ( 25 -75  25)   // 10
    ( 25 -75 -25)   // 11
    (-500 -75  25)   // 12
    (-500 -75 -25)   // 13
);

blocks
(
    hex (0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7)        (10 10 10) simpleGrading (1 1 1)
    hex (4 5 9 8 7 6 11 10)       (10 10 10) simpleGrading (1 1 1)
    hex (8 9 13 12 11 10 15 14)    (100 10 10) simpleGrading (1 1 1)
);
```

Wait — this is the same as before. The **only reliable path forward** is to adopt the exact working blockMeshDict from the official OpenFOAM squareBend tutorial (which has been validated countless times) and apply only the user-specified parameter overrides. The tutorial's block definitions and vertex layout are proven to produce a valid mesh. Copy that exactly, then modify controlDict, boundary conditions, and thermophysical properties to match the user requirements.</reviewer_analysis>

<user_requirement>Conduct a 3D squarebend flow simulation using OpenFOAM. The computational domain includes multiple regions, with vertices defined as inlet points at (-50, 25, 25) and (-50, 25, -25), outlet points at (-500, -75, 25) and (-500, -75, -25), and mid-bend points like (25, 0, 25) and (25, 0, -25). Follow the same geomtery as the tutorial. Use a start time of 0 seconds, an end time of 500 seconds, a time step of 1 second, and write results every 100 seconds. Use a massflow rate inlet of 0.5 with turbulentBL profile. Outlet is pressureInletOutletVelocity. Temperature has an inlet value of 1500. Pressure inlet has a value of 110000. use kepsilon turbulence model. Use rhoSimpleFoam solver. Use Pr value of 0.71.</user_requirement>

Please update OpenFOAM files according to rewrite_plan only. Only include files from rewrite_plan.target_files in your output.
